content.text: Abuse detection identifies when users or bots are exploiting an LLM application in ways that violate usage policies — jailbreaking, data extraction, automated scraping, credential sharing, or cost fraud. Unlike access control, which operates at the gate, abuse detection operates on observed behavior after the request is admitted.

attrs.alt: Three abuse signal sources: volume signals, content signals, and behavioral signals — all feeding into the detection engine

content.text: Volume-Based Detection
The most reliable early signal of abuse is anomalous request volume. Legitimate users have predictable usage patterns; automated abuse tools typically burst at rates far above normal:
Content-Based Detection
Content signals detect abuse by examining what the user is asking. Two techniques complement each other:
Rule-Based Pattern Matching

import re
from enum import Enum

class AbuseCategory(str, Enum):
    JAILBREAK   = 'jailbreak'
    EXTRACTION  = 'extraction'
    HARMFUL     = 'harmful_content'
    SCRAPING    = 'automated_scraping'

CONTENT_RULES: list[tuple[AbuseCategory, re.Pattern]] = [
    # Jailbreak signals
    (AbuseCategory.JAILBREAK, re.compile(
        r'ignore\s+(all\s+)?previous\s+instructions', re.I)),
    (AbuseCategory.JAILBREAK, re.compile(
        r'(DAN|jailbreak|developer\s+mode|god\s+mode)', re.I)),
    (AbuseCategory.JAILBREAK, re.compile(
        r'you\s+are\s+now\s+(a|an|in\s+)', re.I)),
    # Extraction probes
    (AbuseCategory.EXTRACTION, re.compile(
        r'repeat\s+your\s+system\s+prompt', re.I)),
    (AbuseCategory.EXTRACTION, re.compile(
        r'what\s+(were|are)\s+your\s+instructions', re.I)),
    (AbuseCategory.EXTRACTION, re.compile(
        r'reveal\s+.{0,20}(prompt|instructions|context)', re.I)),
    # Bulk / scraping patterns
    (AbuseCategory.SCRAPING, re.compile(
        r'(list|enumerate|give me all|extract all)\s+.{0,30}(items|records|entries)', re.I)),
]

def content_rule_check(text: str) -> list[AbuseCategory]:
    matched = []
    for category, pattern in CONTENT_RULES:
        if pattern.search(text) and category not in matched:
            matched.append(category)
    return matched
LLM-as-Abuse-Classifier
Behavioral Pattern Detection
Behavioral signals look beyond individual requests to patterns across sessions and accounts:
Aggregating Signals into an Abuse Score

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AbuseSignal:
    user_id: str
    volume_flags: list[str]
    content_flags: list[str]
    behavioral_flags: list[str]

    @property
    def score(self) -> float:
        # Weight each signal category
        score = 0.0
        score += len(self.volume_flags) * 0.2
        score += len(self.content_flags) * 0.5   # content is higher signal
        score += len(self.behavioral_flags) * 0.3
        return min(score, 1.0)

    @property
    def severity(self) -> str:
        s = self.score
        if s >= 0.8: return 'critical'
        if s >= 0.5: return 'high'
        if s >= 0.2: return 'medium'
        return 'low'

def evaluate_abuse(
    user_id: str,
    request_text: str,
    tokens_used: int,
    ip: str,
    user_agent: str,
    volume_detector: VolumeAnomalyDetector,
    behavioral_detector: BehavioralAnomalyDetector,
) -> AbuseSignal:
    vol = volume_detector.record(user_id, tokens_used)
    content = content_abuse_check(request_text)
    beh = behavioral_detector.observe(RequestEvent(
        user_id=user_id,
        timestamp=time.time(),
        ip=ip,
        user_agent=user_agent,
        tokens=tokens_used,
    ))

    return AbuseSignal(
        user_id=user_id,
        volume_flags=vol['flags'],
        content_flags=content.get('rule_flags', []) + content.get('llm_flags', []),
        behavioral_flags=beh,
    )
Response Actions
Detected abuse should trigger graduated responses, not binary block/allow decisions. False positives are costly — legitimate users wrongly blocked will churn:
Severity
Typical flags
Response
Low (score < 0.2)
Single volume spike
Log only; no user-visible action
Medium (0.2–0.5)
Content rule hit or behavioral anomaly
Soft throttle; add CAPTCHA; flag for review
Content + volume flags together
Rate-limit to 1 req/min; require re-authentication
Critical (≥ 0.8)
Jailbreak attempt + bot timing + high volume
Block session; suspend account; alert on-call
Summary
LLM abuse detection operates on three orthogonal signal dimensions:
Volume signals
— request rate, token consumption, and session length anomalies detected via sliding-window z-scores.
Content signals
— jailbreak patterns, extraction probes, and harmful content requests detected by rule matching and an LLM classifier.
Behavioral signals
— IP diversity, user-agent churn, and bot-like request timing that indicate credential sharing or automation.

Aggregate these signals into a scored severity level and apply graduated responses — logging at low severity, throttling at medium, blocking at critical. Avoid binary block/allow to minimize false positives on legitimate high-volume users.
